cues.x: How I Built This, by Guy Raz.

Imagine for a moment that you are driving to work, or you are lacing up your shoes at the gym, and someone leans over and asks you a deceptively simple question: how much of a great success story is luck, and how much is grit and skill?
That single question is the beating heart of this book, and it is the question Guy Raz has spent years chasing.

Guy Raz is the journalist and broadcaster behind the wildly popular podcast also called How I Built This, a show on which he has sat across from hundreds of the founders behind companies you use every single day.
He has heard the origin stories of the people who built the beer in your fridge, the shoes on your feet, the app you used to book a place to stay last summer.
And after all those conversations, after all those hours of listening to people describe the moment their whole life pivoted, he distilled what he learned into this book.
It is not a rigid step-by-step manual.

It is closer to a map of a journey, the journey nearly every founder travels, laid out as a series of stages, and illustrated with the real, messy, often terrifying stories of the people who lived it.

So settle in, because we are going to walk that entire road together, from the first flicker of an idea all the way to the far side of success, and we are going to meet some unforgettable people along the way.
Let us start with that central question, because Guy Raz does not dodge it, and neither should we.
When you look at someone who built a company worth hundreds of millions or even billions of dollars, the easy thing to believe is one of two stories.
The first story is that they were simply lucky.
They were in the right place at the right time, they knew the right people, they caught a wave that was going to crest with or without them.

The second story is the opposite, the one Silicon Valley loves to tell about itself, that these founders were geniuses, visionaries, singular talents who willed their empires into existence through sheer brilliance and relentless work.
Raz's honest answer, the one he arrives at after all those interviews, is that both stories are true at the same time, and that is the uncomfortable, beautiful truth.
Luck is real, and it is enormous.

The timing of when you launch, the family you were born into, the mentor who happened to answer your email, the door that happened to be open on the day you knocked: these things matter tremendously, and pretending otherwise is a kind of dishonesty.
But here is the part that should make you sit up a little straighter.
Luck is the part you cannot control.
You cannot schedule it, you cannot summon it, you cannot earn it directly.
What you can control is your grit and your skill and your preparation.

And so the entire philosophy of this book comes down to a quiet, powerful idea: pour your energy into the part you can control, so that when luck finally shows up, and it will, you are already the kind of person, running the kind of thing, that is worth being lucky about.
We will come back to this at the very end, because it is the note the whole book resolves on.
But hold it in your mind as we go.
Before we meet the founders, we have to clear away a myth, because it quietly poisons a lot of people's thinking before they even begin.
The myth is that great founders are chasing money.
Culture tells us this constantly.
We picture the entrepreneur as someone hungry for wealth, someone who wants the big exit, the yacht, the number in the bank account.
And Raz found, over and over, that this is almost exactly backwards.
The founders who lasted, the ones who built things that endured, were not primarily driven by money.
They were driven by purpose.

They had an idea that they simply could not let go of, an itch they could not stop scratching, a problem in the world that bothered them so much they were willing to reorganize their entire life around solving it.
And here is why that matters in a hard, practical, non-sentimental way.
The road of building something is long.
It is punishing.
It takes years, and for most of those years there is very little money and very little glory.
If money is your only fuel, you will run out.
You will hit month eighteen with an empty bank account and no reason left to keep going, and you will quit, and quitting will feel completely rational.
But if purpose is your fuel, if you genuinely cannot stand the idea of the problem going unsolved, you have a reason to get up on the mornings when the money says stop.
Money is a terrible fuel for a long journey because it burns out fast.
Purpose is what gets you across the desert.
So when you hear these founder stories, listen for the purpose underneath them, because that is the engine.
Now, once you have an idea you cannot let go of, the very next question is the one that terrifies everyone: do I leap?
Do I quit my job and bet everything?
And here is where Raz makes one of the most useful distinctions in the whole book, the difference between the calculated leap and the reckless one.
Our culture romanticizes the reckless leap.
We love the story of the person who maxed out every credit card, sold the house, burned the boats, and bet it all on a dream with nothing to fall back on.
It makes a great movie.
But Raz found that the most durable founders almost never did that.
They built a platform first.
And nobody illustrates this better than Jim Koch, the founder of the Boston Beer Company, the man behind Samuel Adams.
Jim Koch came from a family of brewers.
His father was a brewmaster, his grandfather was a brewmaster, going back generations.
And yet Jim did not just rush into beer.
He went to Harvard, he earned degrees, and he took a job at the Boston Consulting Group, one of the most prestigious and well-paid consulting firms in the world.
He spent years there.
He was good at it.
He was making excellent money advising companies.
And the whole time, this idea kept gnawing at him: that American beer had become bland, mass-produced, flavorless, and that he could brew something better using his family's old recipe.
Now here is the crucial part.
When Jim Koch finally decided to leap, he did not do it as a broke, desperate gamble.
He had spent years building skills, understanding business deeply, and he had saved money.
He had a financial cushion.
He left a safe, lucrative career, yes, and that took real courage, but he did not set himself on fire to prove his commitment.
Koch has a wonderful way of putting this.
He says the popular image of the entrepreneur is someone who bets everything, who has no safety net, and he says that is exactly wrong.
His lesson is that you build the platform first, and then you jump from a position of some strength.
You do not need to make yourself destitute to prove you are serious.
Recklessness is not a virtue.
Preparation is.
He had the recipe, he had the knowledge, he had the savings, and then he stepped off.
That is the calculated leap.
And it worked: Samuel Adams became the beer that helped launch the entire American craft brewing revolution.
Contrast that with the mythology, and you start to see how dangerous the reckless leap really is.

For every story of someone who bet everything and won, there are countless stories, mostly untold because failure does not get a podcast episode, of people who bet everything and lost, and had nothing to catch them.
The lesson from Jim Koch is not to be timid.
It is to be smart about your courage.
Give yourself a runway.
Build the platform.
And then leap with everything you have got.
Now let me tell you the story that, more than any other in this book, shows what grit and purpose and cleverness look like when you have almost nothing else.
This is the story of Sara Blakely and Spanx, and I want to tell it in full, because every beat of it teaches something.
Sara Blakely did not come from money, and she did not come from the business world.
She was selling fax machines door to door in Florida.
Think about that job for a second.

You wake up, you put on a suit in the Florida heat, and you walk into office after office where people do not want to see you, and you try to sell them a fax machine, and you get the door shut in your face over and over and over again.
It is one of the hardest, most rejection-soaked jobs there is.
But here is what it did for her: it made her utterly unafraid of the word no.
She heard no so many times that it stopped having power over her.
That turned out to be the single most valuable skill she would ever develop, more valuable than any business degree, and she got it selling fax machines.
The idea for Spanx came from a completely ordinary frustration.
Sara wanted to wear white pants to a party, and she did not like how she looked in them, and she did not have the right undergarment.
So she took a pair of her own pantyhose and cut the feet off, so she could wear them under the pants with open-toed shoes, smoothing everything out without the ugly seam at the foot.
And she looked at that improvised, cut-up pair of pantyhose and had the thought that changes lives: why does this not exist?
Why can nobody buy this?
She had five thousand dollars in savings.
That was it.
That was the whole war chest.
Not five million, not a venture fund, five thousand dollars saved from selling fax machines.
And she decided to build a company on it.
Now, she knew nothing about the hosiery industry, nothing about patents, nothing about manufacturing.
So she did the thing that grit actually looks like, which is not glamorous at all.
She started calling hosiery mills, the factories in North Carolina that actually make this stuff, and she pitched them her idea, this footless, body-shaping garment.
And they rejected her.
One after another.
The mills were run almost entirely by men, and here was a woman with no industry experience describing a product they did not understand and did not believe anyone wanted.
They turned her down flat, again and again.
And then came one of those moments that is equal parts luck and persistence, and you cannot really separate the two.
One mill owner, after initially dismissing her like all the others, went home and mentioned this strange idea to his daughters.
And his daughters lit up.
They said, that is brilliant, we would absolutely wear that.
And on the strength of his own daughters vouching for the idea, that mill owner called Sara back and agreed to make her product.
Now notice what happened there.
The luck was real: it happened to be a man with daughters the right age, who happened to mention it at dinner.
But the luck only mattered because Sara had put herself in front of enough doors that one of them eventually opened.
If she had stopped at the tenth rejection, the daughters never would have heard of it.
Grit is what keeps you standing in front of the door long enough for luck to walk through it.
She could not afford a lawyer to write the patent, which normally costs many thousands of dollars.
So she bought a book on patents and wrote it herself, teaching herself the legal language from a textbook, filing her own patent application.
Sit with that.
She authored her own patent because she could not afford anyone else to.
That is what five thousand dollars and relentless resourcefulness looks like in practice.
But the moment that truly made the company, the moment I want you to remember, happened at Neiman Marcus.
Sara managed to get a meeting with a buyer at Neiman Marcus, the high-end department store.
And the buyer was polite but noncommittal, the way buyers are with unproven products.
So Sara did something audacious.
She said, will you just come to the ladies' room with me?
And she took the buyer into the bathroom, and she changed, showing her own body in a pair of cream-colored pants, first without Spanx and then with Spanx.
The difference was immediate and obvious and undeniable.
The buyer could see it with her own eyes.
And on the spot, she agreed to put Spanx in a handful of Neiman Marcus stores.
That bathroom demonstration is the hinge of the entire company.
Sara understood something crucial: her product could not be explained, it had to be shown.
And she was willing to do something slightly embarrassing, slightly outrageous, to make sure it got shown.
From there, Spanx exploded.
Sara Blakely built it without ever taking on outside investors, without spending money on traditional advertising in the early days, growing it through word of mouth and sheer will.
And she became, for a time, the youngest self-made female billionaire in the world.
From five thousand dollars and a cut-up pair of pantyhose.
Now, when you tell that story, it is tempting to make it sound inevitable, like of course it worked.
But at every single step it could have died.

If she had not learned fearlessness from selling fax machines, if she had quit after the mills rejected her, if that one owner had not had daughters, if she had not had the nerve to walk a buyer into a bathroom, there is no Spanx.
It was grit meeting luck, over and over, at every stage.
Now, Sara Blakely's story also teaches something Raz returns to again and again, and it is a splash of cold water for anyone in love with their own brilliant idea.
The idea itself is maybe five percent of the whole thing.
Maybe less.
Cutting the feet off pantyhose was a clever insight, sure, but it was not some untouchable act of genius that nobody else on earth could have had.
Plenty of women had probably improvised something similar in front of a mirror.
The other ninety-five percent, the part that actually built a company, was execution, and timing, and above all surviving what Raz and many founders call the valley of death.

The valley of death is that long, dark stretch after you have started but before you have succeeded, when the money is running out, when nobody believes in you yet, when every rational signal is telling you to give up.
Most companies die in the valley of death, not because the idea was bad, but because the founders could not survive long enough to get to the other side.
So the real skill, Raz argues, is not having the idea.
Everyone has ideas.
The skill is execution and endurance, the ability to keep the thing alive through the valley when there is no proof yet that it will work.
And no story captures the valley of death more vividly than Airbnb.
Picture two young guys in San Francisco, Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia.
They are roommates, they are creative types, they went to design school, and they cannot make rent.
That is the starting point.
Not a grand vision of disrupting the global hotel industry.
Just two guys who are broke and about to get squeezed by their landlord.
There is a big design conference coming to San Francisco, and all the hotels in the city are booked solid.
And they have a thought: what if they blew up a few air mattresses in their apartment and rented out floor space to conference attendees who could not find a room?
They put up a quick website.
They called it Air Bed and Breakfast, literally, because it was air mattresses and they would make breakfast.
A few strangers actually showed up, slept on air mattresses on their floor, and paid them.
And Brian and Joe looked at each other and thought, wait, there might be something bigger here.
But knowing there might be something bigger and actually building it are separated by a canyon, and that canyon nearly killed them.
They brought in a third co-founder, a technical guy named Nathan Blecharczyk, to build the real product.
And then they spent a brutal stretch of time where almost nobody used it and almost nobody believed in it.
They pitched investors relentlessly.
And they got rejected relentlessly.
Investor after investor told them the idea was crazy.
Who on earth would want to sleep in a stranger's home?
Who would want strangers sleeping in theirs?
It sounded unsafe, unscalable, faintly ridiculous.
Raz has shared the actual rejection emails from investors, and they are almost comically dismissive, polite versions of "this will never work."
The founders were racking up debt to stay alive, tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt spread across a wallet full of cards, just to keep the lights on and keep coding and keep going.
Brian Chesky has talked about maxing out card after card.
They were, by any normal financial measure, failing.
And here is where the Airbnb story becomes legendary, because of how they survived the valley of death.
It was two thousand eight.
It was a presidential election year, Barack Obama against John McCain.
And these broke founders, unable to raise money the normal way, came up with an absolutely audacious hustle.
They decided to make novelty breakfast cereal.
Not as the business, understand, but purely to raise emergency cash to fund the business.
They designed limited-edition election-themed cereal boxes: Obama O's, "the breakfast of change," and Cap'n McCain's, "a maverick in every bite."

They could not afford to have real cereal boxes manufactured, so they got a small batch of plain boxes and folded and assembled them by hand, gluing them together late into the night, filling them with generic cereal, numbering each one as a limited collector's edition.
And they sold these cereal boxes, riding the wave of election-year attention, and they made something in the neighborhood of thirty thousand dollars.
Thirty thousand dollars from handmade novelty cereal.
And that money, that ridiculous, improvised, non-scalable cereal money, is what kept Airbnb alive long enough to reach the point where the real business finally caught fire.
Think about what that story actually demonstrates.
It is not the air mattress idea that saved Airbnb.
It is the willingness to do anything, anything at all, no matter how undignified or off-strategy, to survive one more month in the valley of death.
In fact, that cereal hustle became the thing that finally impressed the famous startup accelerator Y Combinator and its founder Paul Graham.
He was not impressed by the travel idea at first.
He was impressed that these guys had gotten people to pay forty dollars for a box of cereal that cost almost nothing.
He looked at that and thought, if they can convince people to do that, they are the kind of relentless people who will not die.
And that quality, not the idea, is what he bet on.
Airbnb went on to become a company worth tens of billions of dollars, one that genuinely transformed how the whole world travels.
But it lived only because two broke guys refused to let it die and were willing to fold cereal boxes by hand to buy time.
That willingness to survive at any cost brings us to another founder whose story is almost the pure distillation of endurance: James Dyson and his vacuum cleaner.
Now, we all know Dyson vacuums today, those sleek, expensive, bagless machines.
But the story of how that vacuum came to exist is a story about a number, and the number is five thousand one hundred and twenty-seven.
James Dyson was frustrated, the way so many founders start, by an everyday annoyance.
His vacuum cleaner kept losing suction as the bag filled up.
The bag would clog, the airflow would choke, and the thing would stop working properly.
And he thought there had to be a better way, a vacuum that did not use a bag at all, that used cyclonic separation to spin the dust out of the air.
It was a good idea.
But an idea, remember, is five percent.

The other ninety-five percent, for Dyson, meant building prototype after prototype after prototype in a workshop, refining the design a tiny bit each time, testing it, failing, adjusting, trying again.
He built five thousand one hundred and twenty-six prototypes that did not work well enough.
Five thousand one hundred and twenty-six failures.
And the five thousand one hundred and twenty-seventh was the one that finally worked.
This took years.
Years.
And during those years he went deep into debt, nearly bankrupt, supported through much of it by his wife's income as an art teacher.
He was, by conventional standards, a man throwing his life away on a vacuum cleaner that did not work, for year after year after year.
And it did eventually work, spectacularly.

Dyson became a household name and James Dyson became a billionaire, and his story is now told as the ultimate proof that persistence pays off, that if you just refuse to give up you will eventually break through.
And that is true.
But Raz is too honest to leave it there, and this is one of the most important and least comfortable ideas in the entire book, so I want to slow down on it.
Here is the hard truth about James Dyson and his five thousand one hundred and twenty-seven prototypes.
We tell that story as a triumph of grit because it worked.
But grit is not the same thing as stubbornness, and the difference between them is invisible until the very end.
For every James Dyson who persevered through five thousand failures and was right, there is someone out there, many someones, who persevered through five thousand failures and were simply wrong.

People who poured a decade and their family's savings and their whole identity into an idea that was never going to work, and who could not see it, and who kept going out of stubbornness rather than grit, and who lost everything.
Those stories do not get told.
They do not get a podcast episode.
They do not become inspirational.
But they are just as common, maybe more common.
So the question that should haunt every founder is this: how do I know whether I am a Dyson or one of the invisible people who persevered and were wrong?
Raz's answer to that is one of the most useful pieces of wisdom in the book, and it is a distinction I want you to really hold onto.
The answer is that you cling to the dream, but you change the plan.
The founders who succeed are not the ones who stubbornly refuse to change anything.
They are the ones who are absolutely committed to the destination, the purpose, the problem they are trying to solve, but who are ruthlessly flexible about the route they take to get there.
James Dyson did not build the same failed prototype five thousand times.
He built five thousand different prototypes, each one adjusted based on what the last one taught him.
He was flexible on the plan, iterating constantly, while remaining fixed on the dream of a bagless vacuum.
That is grit.
Stubbornness, by contrast, is refusing to change anything, insisting the world is wrong and you are right, building the same broken thing over and over and blaming the market.
Grit is persistent about the goal and endlessly adaptive about the method.
Stubbornness is rigid about everything.
The difference is subtle from the inside, which is exactly why so many people get it wrong.

The lesson is to hold your vision tightly and hold your specific plan loosely, and to actually listen to the feedback the world is giving you, because sometimes persevering is the right call and sometimes quitting is the wisest, bravest thing you can do.
Knowing which is which is the whole game, and there is no formula for it, only honesty.
Now, there is a quieter thread running through all of these stories, one that does not get the headlines but that Raz found in interview after interview, and it is this: almost nobody does it alone.
We love the myth of the solo genius, the lone founder against the world.
But look closely at the stories.

Airbnb was three people, and the friendship and complementary skills between Brian, Joe, and Nathan were what let them survive the valley of death together, because when one of them was despairing, the others could hold the line.
James Dyson had his wife earning the income that kept the family afloat through all those years of failure, and he has said plainly that he could not have done it without her.

Behind so many of these founders is a co-founder who balanced their weaknesses, or a business partner who brought the skill they lacked, or a spouse who believed in the dream when the bank account said it was insane and who kept the household running so the founder could keep going.
Raz emphasizes this because the emotional weight of building something is crushing, and the financial risk is often shared by someone who never gets any of the credit.
The right co-founder is not just extra labor, it is emotional survival.

When you are in the valley of death and you have maxed out your credit cards and every investor has said no, the difference between quitting and continuing is very often whether there is another human being standing next to you who still believes.

So one of the most practical pieces of advice buried in these inspiring stories is deeply unromantic: choose your partners, in business and in life, with enormous care, because you are going to be in the trenches with them, and the trenches are longer and darker than you can imagine from the outside.

The supportive spouse, the loyal co-founder, the partner who takes the salaried job so the dream can breathe: these people are load-bearing walls in almost every success story, even the ones that get told as solo triumphs.
And underneath the co-founders and the partners, underneath the grit and the endurance, we come back to purpose, because purpose is what makes the whole structure hold together over the long haul.

Remember Jim Koch left a job that paid extremely well to brew beer, and it was not because he calculated that beer would make him richer than consulting, because it almost certainly would not have, on paper.
It was because he came from generations of brewers and he could not stand what American beer had become and he had to make it better.
That is purpose.
Sara Blakely was not chasing a billion dollars when she cut the feet off her pantyhose.
She was solving a problem she personally had, and she genuinely believed she could help millions of women feel better in their clothes, and that belief carried her through every rejection.
Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia came, over time, to believe they were building a world where people could belong anywhere, where a stranger's home could become a place of trust and connection.
James Dyson wanted, at a level that was almost obsessive, to build a better machine.
In every case, the money came as a consequence of the purpose, never as the goal that drove it.
And that ordering matters, because purpose is what you fall back on when the money is gone, and in the valley of death, the money is always gone.
Money cannot sustain you through five thousand failed prototypes.
Only purpose can.
The founders who tried to build something purely for money tended not to last, because the moment it got hard, and it always gets hard, there was nothing underneath to hold them up.
So let us pull all of these threads together, because we have traveled the whole road now, and it is worth standing at the end of it and looking back at the map.
We started with an idea you cannot let go of, driven not by money but by purpose, because money is a fuel that burns out and purpose is the fuel that gets you across the desert.
We watched Jim Koch teach us to take the calculated leap and not the reckless one, to build the platform first and not set ourselves on fire to prove our commitment.

We watched Sara Blakely turn five thousand dollars and a fearlessness learned from selling fax machines into Spanx, reminding us that the idea is only five percent and that execution, resourcefulness, and the nerve to walk a buyer into a bathroom are the rest.
We watched Brian and Joe fold cereal boxes by hand to drag Airbnb through the valley of death, because surviving one more month is often the only strategy that matters.

We watched James Dyson build five thousand one hundred and twenty-seven prototypes, and we learned the razor's edge between grit and stubbornness, that we must cling to the dream but change the plan, that persevering is sometimes wisdom and sometimes folly, and that honesty about which is which is the hardest and most important skill of all.
And we saw, underneath every one of these stories, a co-founder or a partner or a spouse in the trenches, because almost no one does this alone.
Which brings us, finally, back to the question we started with, the one that has been humming underneath this entire journey.
How much of this is luck, and how much is grit and skill?
And now that we have walked the road, Guy Raz's honest answer lands with real weight.
It is both.
It is genuinely both, and anyone who tells you it is only one is selling you something.
Luck is real and it is enormous.
The timing of Airbnb launching just as the world was ready to trust strangers online.
The mill owner who happened to have daughters the right age.
The election year that made novelty cereal sellable.
The family you were born into, the mentor who answered, the door that happened to be open on the day you knocked.
All of that is luck, and it is vast, and it is humbling, and to pretend you succeeded on grit alone is a kind of arrogance and a kind of lie.
But, and this is the whole point, this is where the book leaves you standing: luck is the part you cannot control.
You cannot manufacture it, you cannot schedule it, you cannot earn it directly by wanting it more.
So it makes no sense at all to spend your energy there.

Spend your energy on the part you can control, which is your grit, your skill, your preparation, your willingness to survive the valley of death, your honesty about when to persevere and when to change the plan, your care in choosing the people beside you, your loyalty to a purpose deeper than money.
Do all of that, relentlessly, for years, not because it guarantees success, because nothing guarantees success, but for a subtler and more powerful reason.

Do it so that when luck finally shows up, and if you keep standing in front of enough doors it eventually will, you are already the kind of person, running the kind of thing, that is worth being lucky about.
The mill owner's daughters only mattered because Sara had already knocked on a hundred doors.
The election year only mattered because Brian and Joe were already there, broke and building, ready to fold the boxes.
Luck rewards the prepared, not because life is fair, but because luck is useless to someone who has not done the work to be ready to catch it.
So if you take one thing from this book as you finish your drive or your workout, let it be this.
Fall in love with a problem, not with money.
Build your platform, then leap with courage but not recklessness.
Understand that your brilliant idea is only five percent, and pour yourself into the ninety-five percent, which is execution and endurance.
When you hit the valley of death, and you will, do whatever it takes, however undignified, to survive one more month.
Cling to your dream and hold your plan loosely, changing the route as often as the world demands while never losing the destination.
Choose the people beside you like your life depends on it, because in a way it does.
And then let go of the part you cannot control.
Stop waiting to be lucky.
Start becoming the kind of person luck would be lucky to find.
That is the unexpected path to success, the one Guy Raz found hiding inside hundreds of stories, and it turns out to be the same path, walked by very different people, every single time.
The door will open when it opens.
Your only job, the whole of your job, is to make sure that when it does, you are standing right there, ready, having built something worth walking through it for.
